region_of_origin: Indonesia (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic meatball food product class; no Indonesia-specific bakso class exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers meat-based prepared dishes; no bakso-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bakso or Indonesian meatball in the published SNOMED CT international release.

preparation_method: ground meat mixed with tapioca flour and seasonings

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bakso as an add-on consists of 3 meatball pieces weighing approximately 75 g total (approximately 25 g per piece).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Meatballs, beef, cooked (SR Legacy ID 21133); values cross-validated against input record and adjusted for tapioca flour dilution characteristic of Indonesian bakso preparation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact bakso entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as a standalone record. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef meatball (ID 21133) was used as the primary approved anchor. Input record values (energy 140 kcal, protein 12.67 g, fat 7.33 g, carbohydrate 5.33 g, sodium 466.67 mg, saturated fat 3.33 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy beef meatball composition adjusted for tapioca flour dilution. Input values retained with USDA SR Legacy as the supporting approved anchor. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water set to -1 as detailed values are not reported in the closest USDA SR Legacy match for this preparation. The input record's listed source URL (foodo.sg) was not used as a nutrient evidence source because the input nutrient values were AI-generated from GrabFood delivery platform data rather than directly extracted from the HPB Foodo database; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate for meat-starch mixed dish based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable meat-based dishes with starch binder.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for bakso exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) low carbohydrate content (5.33 g/100 g), predominantly from tapioca flour which has a moderate-to-high GI in isolation but is substantially attenuated by the high protein and fat matrix; (2) protein-fat matrix suppresses glycaemic response; (3) closest analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database for meat-starch mixed dishes (e.g., meat dumplings, meat-filled pastries) cluster in the GI 35–45 range per Atkinson et al. 2008. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (40 × 5.33 × 0.75) / 100 = 1.6, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 466.67 mg/100 g; a 75 g serving delivers approximately 350 mg sodium, representing approximately 15–17% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit bakso to 2–3 pieces per occasion, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid consuming the accompanying broth, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.33 g/100 g), very low sugar (0.67 g/100 g), high protein (12.67 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~40) support stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bakso is a suitable protein-rich, low-carbohydrate option for individuals with type 2 diabetes; limit portion to 3 pieces, avoid high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as noodles or rice in large quantities, and choose reduced-sodium preparation to manage comorbid hypertension risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.33 g/100 g, trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g, cholesterol at 33.33 mg/100 g, and high sodium at 466.67 mg/100 g are collectively relevant to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume bakso in moderation (3 pieces maximum per serving), prefer chicken-based over beef-based bakso to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid load, avoid the broth to limit sodium, and ensure overall dietary saturated fat remains within recommended limits (<7% of total energy).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef-based bakso contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA; high sodium further exacerbates joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid beef-based bakso and consider chicken or fish-based meatball alternatives; request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to minimise sodium-driven joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso is a traditional Indonesian meatball made from finely ground beef or chicken mixed with tapioca flour, salt, garlic, white pepper, and water, then shaped into smooth round balls and boiled until cooked through. The texture is firm yet springy due to the tapioca starch binder, and the flavour is savoury and mildly seasoned. Bakso is one of the most popular street foods and restaurant add-ons across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore, commonly served in a clear broth alongside noodles, tofu, and fried shallots, or as a standalone side dish. A standard serving of 3 pieces weighs approximately 75 g. Nutritionally, bakso is a high-protein (12.67 g/100 g), moderate-fat (7.33 g/100 g), low-carbohydrate (5.33 g/100 g) food with an estimated low glycaemic index of approximately 40. Sodium content is elevated at 466.67 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100 g) are present at moderate levels. Bakso is generally considered halal when prepared with beef or chicken. It is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but sodium management is critical for those with hypertension or heart disease. Beef-based bakso is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 3 pieces of bakso?
Can I eat bakso if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact bakso entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values from the input record (originally AI-generated from GrabFood data, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef meatball values (ID 21133) adjusted for tapioca flour dilution; USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved anchor. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for meat-starch mixed dishes per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for bakso (Indonesian meatball with tapioca flour) are not available as a distinct entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — bakso contains no plant-derived vitamin C sources; consistent with USDA SR Legacy beef meatball data). All core macros (protein_g 12.67, fat_g 7.33, carbohydrate_g 5.33) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a boiled meat-starch meatball. The input record listed foodo.sg as the source URL but the nutrient values were AI-generated from GrabFood delivery platform data; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and was not used as nutrient evidence. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ID 21133) is the approved nutrient anchor. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for bakso is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — check passed.
